config.title: Egypt
config.destination: Cairo, Luxor, Aswan, and the Nile
config.coverNote: I built this as the first Carta shape for Egypt: Cairo and Giza with the Grand Egyptian Museum, Luxor with enough time to actually understand what you are seeing, Aswan as the softer finish, and a Nile rhythm that keeps the trip from becoming a race through monuments. Egypt is not the place where I would leave you with a PDF and wish you luck. The guide, driver, hotel base, early starts, and food stops are the trip.

days.title: Arrive in Cairo
days.summary: Land, settle near the Nile or Giza, and keep dinner easy. The first win in Egypt is not over-scheduling arrival day.

days.title: Giza and the Grand Egyptian Museum
days.summary: Pyramids early, a protected pyramid-view meal, then GEM while there is still energy to absorb it.

days.title: Saqqara, Dahshur, and Cairo food
days.summary: Use the second Cairo day for context and a better food night, not another generic checklist.

days.title: Fly to Luxor
days.summary: Temple rhythm starts here. Karnak is strongest when the day still feels quiet.

days.title: Luxor West Bank
days.summary: Valley of the Kings, Medinet Habu, and fewer tombs seen properly. This is where I would protect the guide quality.

days.title: Board the Nile
days.summary: The boat is not just transport. It is the pacing device that keeps Egypt from feeling like a transfer spreadsheet.

days.title: Nile temples and slow water
days.summary: Kom Ombo, Edfu, and time on deck. The silence between sites is part of the trip.

days.title: Aswan
days.summary: Philae, a felucca, and a softer Nile evening. Aswan is where the trip exhales.

days.title: Abu Simbel
days.summary: Very early start, high payoff. Worth it for clients who came for scale and history.

days.title: Return Cairo or add Red Sea
days.summary: Either protect the international departure with a Cairo night or extend three nights to the Red Sea.

itinerary.title: Private airport meet-and-assist
itinerary.subtitle: Cairo International Airport to hotel
itinerary.personalNote: This is one of the places I would not try to save money. Arrival in Cairo should feel handled from the first minute: bags, visa backup, driver, hotel check-in, and no taxi negotiation while you are tired.

itinerary.title: Pyramids of Giza and Grand Egyptian Museum
itinerary.subtitle: Private Egyptologist, early start
itinerary.personalNote: The pyramids are the obvious headline, but the day can go wrong in the logistics around them. I would keep this guided, start early, and treat GEM as its own major experience rather than the tired last stop.

details.Watch-out: Confirm current Giza entrance and shuttle rules

itinerary.title: Fly Cairo to Luxor
itinerary.subtitle: Morning flight preferred
itinerary.personalNote: For most clients, I would fly this leg. The romance of overland travel is not worth spending a full day tired before Luxor, where early mornings matter.

itinerary.title: Board dahabiya or small Nile vessel
itinerary.subtitle: Luxor to Aswan rhythm
itinerary.personalNote: I would rather pick the right boat than the most decorated brochure. The Nile segment should slow the whole trip down, not become another hotel with a schedule taped to the wall.

itinerary.title: Abu Simbel
itinerary.subtitle: Early private excursion or flight-supported visit
itinerary.personalNote: This is the early start I would defend if the client cares about ancient Egypt. It is far, it is not casual, and that is partly why it lands so hard when you finally stand in front of it.

picks.name: Marriott Mena House
picks.description: The pyramid-view classic at Giza.
picks.note: I would use Mena House for the Giza and GEM segment, not necessarily for the whole Cairo stay. The view is the point, and it is a strong first emotional hit when the room category is right.

picks.name: Four Seasons Hotel Cairo at Nile Plaza
picks.address: 1089 Corniche El Nil, Garden City, Cairo
picks.description: A polished Nile-side Cairo base with spa and city access.
picks.note: This is the practical Cairo answer when the trip needs a city base more than a pyramid-view fantasy. I would use it for central Cairo, Zamalek dinners, museums, and cleaner movement.

picks.name: Sofitel Legend Old Cataract Aswan
picks.description: The historic Nile palace hotel in Aswan.
picks.note: I love the idea of Old Cataract for the right client, but I would be careful with room category and expectations. This should feel like a quiet Nile finale, not a price tag we forgot to question.

picks.name: Al Moudira
picks.description: A quieter West Bank retreat outside central Luxor.
picks.note: This is the Luxor choice when I want the West Bank to feel like part of the trip, not just a morning excursion. It gives space around the temples and tombs.

picks.name: Grand Egyptian Museum
picks.address: Kafr Nassar, Al Haram, Giza Governorate
picks.description: The major new museum near the pyramids, planned around the Tutankhamun collection and Egypt's ancient civilization arc.
picks.note: I would give GEM real space. It is not something to tack onto the end of a hard pyramid day when everyone is already cooked.

picks.name: Pyramids of Giza and the Sphinx
picks.description: The opening act everyone imagines, but it needs timing and protection.
picks.note: Early, guided, and with the current entrance and shuttle rules checked. That is how this becomes awe instead of friction.
picks.hours: Typically daytime hours, verify before travel

picks.name: Saqqara and Dahshur
picks.description: The quieter pyramid context that makes Giza make more sense.
picks.note: I would add this for people who care about the story, not just the photo. It is usually calmer than Giza and often more rewarding than clients expect.

picks.name: Karnak Temple
picks.description: The scale piece in Luxor, best before the day gets loud and hot.
picks.note: I would start early. Karnak is not just another temple, but it loses some power when you are seeing it shoulder to shoulder in the heat.

picks.name: Valley of the Kings
picks.description: The tomb circuit that needs restraint and a strong guide.
picks.note: I would rather choose fewer tombs and understand them than sprint through every possible ticket. This is where pace matters.

picks.name: Philae Temple
picks.description: A graceful temple experience reached by boat near Aswan.
picks.note: This is one of the places where Aswan starts to feel softer than Luxor. I would protect the timing and keep the boat logistics handled.

picks.name: Abu Simbel
picks.description: Remote, monumental, and worth defending for history-focused clients.
picks.note: This is not a casual half-day. It asks something of you. If you came to Egypt for scale, that is why I would include it anyway.

picks.name: Khufu's
picks.description: Contemporary Egyptian food with the pyramid view built into the meal.
picks.note: This is the kind of restaurant that solves a day, because the meal is not separate from the reason you came. Book it around the Giza and GEM plan.
picks.hours: Breakfast and lunch, evening options vary by concept

picks.name: 139 Pavilion
picks.address: Marriott Mena House, 6 Pyramids Road, Giza
picks.description: Open-air Mena House restaurant with a pyramid backdrop.
picks.note: A calm pyramid-view breakfast or lunch here can be more useful than another forced sightseeing stop. Sometimes the right table is the itinerary.

picks.name: Abou El Sid
picks.description: Atmospheric Zamalek restaurant for classic Egyptian dishes.
picks.note: I would use this for the client who wants Egyptian food without turning dinner into a field test. Order molokheya, mezzes, and something that feels like the room.

picks.name: Koshary Abou Tarek
picks.description: The famous downtown koshary stop.
picks.note: Not fancy, not quiet, not romantic. That is why it works. I would make this a quick, specific food stop, not a long dinner plan.

picks.name: Zööba
picks.address: Multiple Cairo locations including Zamalek and GEM
picks.description: A cleaner first-timer introduction to Egyptian street-food staples.
picks.note: This is where I would send someone who wants fuul, taameya, hawawshi, and koshary without making the first bite in Cairo feel risky.

picks.name: Naguib Mahfouz Cafe
picks.description: Trusted atmospheric stop inside Khan el Khalili.
picks.note: If we are doing the market, I want one place where the client can sit, cool down, and not think about whether the kitchen is a gamble.

picks.name: Le Pacha 1901
picks.description: Nile-docked boat with multiple restaurants and an easy evening feel.
picks.note: This is not the deepest food choice in Cairo. It is a useful one: Nile view, options, and a night that does not require a complicated cross-city plan.

picks.name: Crimson
picks.description: Rooftop Nile view with drinks and dinner energy.
picks.note: I would use Crimson for a Cairo evening when the client wants air, a view, and a drink without pretending this is ancient-history programming.

picks.name: Nour El Nil Dahabiya
picks.description: Small-scale dahabiya Nile cruising between Luxor and Aswan.
picks.note: This is the shape I would look at first when the client wants the Nile to feel intimate. The boat should slow the trip down, not make it feel more crowded.

picks.name: Four Seasons Resort Sharm El Sheikh
picks.address: 1 Four Seasons Boulevard, Sharm El Sheikh
picks.description: Major Red Sea resort option with diving, spa, and family infrastructure.
picks.note: I would add Red Sea only if the trip has enough days to breathe. Sharm is the polished resort answer, not the same emotional trip as Cairo and Luxor.

contacts.name: Gabe
contacts.note: Text me first. Anything moves, I move it.

guideBlocks.heading: How I would pace Egypt
guideBlocks.body: I would not plan Egypt by counting sights. I would plan it by protecting attention. Cairo and Giza need control. Luxor needs early starts and fewer tombs done better. Aswan needs quiet. The Nile segment should make the trip breathe, not just move you from one temple to another.

guideBlocks.heading: Guide and driver strategy
guideBlocks.body: Use private guides where Egypt gets high-friction: airport arrival, Giza, Saqqara, Luxor West Bank, Abu Simbel, and any market-heavy day. I would brief the guide before the trip: no forced shopping stops, no rushed tomb circuit, and no vague tipping surprises.

guideBlocks.heading: What to skip if time is tight
guideBlocks.body: If you have seven nights, I would skip the Red Sea and protect Cairo, Luxor, Aswan, and Abu Simbel. If you have ten nights, I would add a proper Nile rhythm. If you have two weeks, then the Red Sea or desert finish starts to make sense.

guideBlocks.heading: Food notes
guideBlocks.body: Cairo food should be specific, not random. I would use Khufu's or 139 Pavilion around Giza, Abou El Sid or Le Pacha for a Zamalek night, Abou Tarek for a fast koshary stop, and Zooba when someone wants the safer first version of Egyptian street food.

guideBlocks.heading: Power and plug compatibility
guideBlocks.body: Egypt commonly uses Type C and Type F plugs, 220V, 50Hz. Most modern phone, laptop, and camera chargers are dual-voltage, but hair tools and specialty devices are often not. Pack a compact universal adapter in your carry-on, and check any U.S. hair tool before bringing it.

essentials.label: Emergency
essentials.note: Ask the hotel or guide to call first if possible. Keep Gabe and the local guide in the same message thread.

essentials.label: Tourist Police
essentials.note: Useful for tourist-specific issues. Confirm current numbers before travel.

essentials.label: U.S. Embassy Cairo
essentials.address: 5 Tawfik Diab Street, Garden City, Cairo

essentials.label: For a taxi driver
essentials.address: فندق ماريوت مينا هاوس، طريق الأهرامات، الجيزة
